title: The media
description: Practice your skills in this quiz on the media's role as a linkage institution
topic_path: How does the media shape our understanding of politics?

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the image below to answer the following question. 

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0d9caa09b6586e5b8281553e4a907077.png)

 Source: [Wikimedia Commons](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Fake_news#/media/File:The_fin_de_si%C3%A8cle_newspaper_proprietor_cropped.jpg)

**Which of the following statements best explains how this 1894 political cartoon of reporters rushing their stories to print applies to news reporting today?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: There is no such thing as completely objective journalism
Today’s journalists are more accountable than in the past
Soft or entertainment news has been mostly eliminated by modern journalists
Past reporters were more concerned about reporting hard news than they are today

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the image below to answer the following question. 

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0d9caa09b6586e5b8281553e4a907077.png)

> Source: [Wikimedia Commons](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Fake_news#/media/File:The_fin_de_si%C3%A8cle_newspaper_proprietor_cropped.jpg)

**Which of the following statements would the cartoon’s author most agree with even in the context of today’s news coverage?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: To ensure accountability by government and news outlets, citizens must learn to distinguish objective and truthful news from biased and false reporting
News media must self-regulate to ensure a focus on serious news, covering important issues and based on facts and figures
The news media are mostly interested in attack journalism, fake news, or biased coverage, which is one of their legitimate functions
More legal restraints on disreputable news sites are necessary in order to maintain democratic processes and hold reporters accountable

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best explains the danger that “attack journalism” poses to political participation? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: By treating government officials and candidates as targets to be stalked in pursuit of scandal, attack journalists create the impression that all politicians are ineffective and untrustworthy

By not providing equal time to candidates of different political parties to speak on the issues, media organizations keep constituents ignorant of important information that could influence an election or policy decision

This media coverage aims to entertain or shock, often through sensationalized reporting or by focusing on a politician’s personality, which turns elections into beauty pageants at the expense of issue debates
This type of election coverage focuses more on poll results and speculation about a likely winner than on substantive differences between candidates, reducing voter interest

assessment_items.question_content:
>". . . without debate, without criticism, no administration and no country can succeed—and no republic can survive . . . that is why our press was protected by the First Amendment--the only business in America specifically protected by the Constitution . . . ”

>President John F. Kennedy, The President and the Press: Address before the American Newspaper Publishers Association, April 27, 1961

>Source: [jfklibrary.org](https://www.jfklibrary.org/Research/Research-Aids/JFK-Speeches/American-Newspaper-Publishers-Association_19610427.aspx)

**Based on the excerpt above, which of the following statements would Kennedy have most likely agreed with in terms of both the ideals of, and challenges for, the US republic?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: No content restrictions should be put on any media sites or companies, as total press freedom will eventually correct any abuses
Cyber attacks and hacking of social media and internet news should be allowed only for national security reasons
First Amendment protections should be limited only to media that refrain from constantly attacking the president and executive office staff
An independent media must provide accurate information and commentary that uphold democratic principles and civil society

assessment_items.question_content:
**Horse race journalism is best defined as which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: When the media focuses on who is winning an election rather than on  policy agendas and debates
When the media sets the political agenda by drawing public and government attention to certain issues
When the media does a deep investigation on a specific topic
When the media publishes stories that exploit or exaggerate the news to attract people

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which linkage institution is primarily responsible for keeping the electorate informed about political issues, policymaking, and leadership transitions in government?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Interest groups
Political parties
The federal bureaucracy
News media

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the text below to answer the following question.

>“If you start from the understanding that important party decisions are made before the voters weigh in, the question 'who’s winning?'—or, alternately, 'who is the party choosing, and which people are making that choice?'—becomes one of the best ways to give ordinary citizens who want to become more active political participants the information they need. (If you’re not a party member, meanwhile, knowing the process by which the party made its choice—rather than just the outcome it arrived at—can still help inform your own political participation.)”

> -Greg Marx, “In Defense of (the Right Kind of) Horse Race Journalism,” *Columbia Journalism Review*, September 6, 2011.

**Regarding the above excerpt, which of the following statements would the author most likely agree with?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Horse race journalism is a more effective tool for informing voters and attracting their participation when applied to primary elections instead of general elections
The right kind of horse race journalism has to do with waiting for voters to make their decision about candidates during the general election campaign based on who is ahead in the race
Journalists should spend more time writing about differences in campaigns as well as predicting winners in tightly contested races a few days before the election
The current generation of political reporters do not have the expertise to explain how party insiders are deciding and debating in a political contest

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the text below to answer the following question.

>“Since polls conducted for the press must be simplified for comprehension by the general public, poll takers typically measure opinions crudely and analyze the data superficially. For example, measurement often involves yes/no, approve/disapprove, or agree/disagree dichotomies rather than finely calibrated scales that would more precisely reflect shades of opinion.”

-Charles Atkins, James Gaudino, “Polling and the Democratic Consensus,” *Annals of the American Academy of Politics and Social Sciences* Vol. 472, 1984, p. 128.

**In reference to the above excerpt, which of the following events most clearly supports the criticism of how public opinion polls are typically used during political campaign news coverage?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The 2016 presidential election
The 2016 Democratic and Republican presidential nomination races
The gubernatorial elections of 2016
The midterm election of 2014

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following impacts on political participation has resulted from the decrease in barriers to publishing on the internet?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It has allowed people with little or no political experience or connections to have significant influence on elections
It has motivated readers to consult a wide range of media sources instead of focusing on sites with similar views as their own
It has led to greater accuracy of political information being produced by unofficial sources
It has made it more difficult for like-minded political supporters to organize and stay informed on issues of interest to them

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the graph below to answer the following question.

**Public trust in government**
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/18e097f8bbd56881e65fa545998926fc.png)

>Source: [Pew Research Center](http://www.people-press.org/2017/05/03/public-trust-in-government-1958-2017/#chart)

**Which of the following media developments from 1958 to 2017 has most likely contributed to the trend in public trust of the federal government?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Increased use of investigative and attack journalism to discredit politicians
Increased consolidation of news outlets into media monopolies
Increased availability of online versions of print newspapers
Increased availability of 24-hour news coverage in cable news networks

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the graph below to answer the following question.

**Public trust in government**
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/18e097f8bbd56881e65fa545998926fc.png)

>Source: [Pew Research Center](http://www.people-press.org/2017/05/03/public-trust-in-government-1958-2017/#chart)

**Which of the following accurately describes a trend of public trust in the federal government from 1958 to 2017?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Public trust has generally declined, rising briefly during periods of economic growth and the 9/11 crisis
The moving average of trust levels has had greater variations than the trust levels derived from individual polls
Public trust has increased during wartime administrations when the nation’s citizens have rallied around the president
Public trust has declined during Democratic presidencies and has risen during Republican administrations

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following scenarios is an example of how the media can act as a linkage institution? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A television network airs the results of public opinion poll projecting the winner of the 2016 presidential election
A political action committee donates money to a candidate for Senate to use for commercial advertisements
A political party advertises a “Rock the Vote” concert on Facebook to get people aged 18-21 to vote
A reporter interviews students and teachers after a school-wide evacuation because of a fire

assessment_items.question_content:
A video of a high school student being denied lunch because of unpaid meal fees goes viral on social media, leading to widespread criticism and the hashtag #LunchShaming. Activists and public figures spread the story, and users begin sharing similar incidents from other schools around the country. As the issue gains attention online, traditional news outlets—including newspapers, television, and digital media—begin covering the story nationwide. In response to the public pressure, several members of Congress release statements and propose legislation to address school lunch debt.

**Which concept best explains the role of digital media in this scenario?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Agenda setting
Horse race journalism
Issue framing
Political efficacy

assessment_items.question_content:
During a presidential election year, major news networks devote most of their political coverage to tracking polls and fundraising totals and making predictions about which candidate is gaining or losing momentum. Reporters rarely analyze differences between the candidates’ positions on major issues. This style of coverage dominates headlines and social media clips, especially in the final weeks before Election Day.

**What is a likely effect of this approach to political reporting?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Public attention shifts from policy substance to campaign strategy and candidate performance.
Voters become more informed about candidates’ proposed policies and positions on key issues.
Media coverage alone determines the election outcome without influence from other political factors.
Third-party candidates receive increased attention and become more competitive in national elections.

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of environmental activists organizes a campaign on social media to raise awareness about water contamination in a rural area. The posts gain national attention, prompting investigative reports from major news outlets. After continued coverage, members of Congress begin holding hearings and proposing legislation to address the issue.

**Which statement best explains the role of the media in this scenario?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The media is acting as a linkage institution by connecting public concerns to policymakers through coverage and agenda-setting.
The media is enforcing environmental regulations through investigative oversight and direct intervention in policy outcomes.
The media is shaping public opinion to align with government policy goals through targeted messaging and selective coverage.
The media is lobbying Congress to pass environmental protection laws by promoting legislation and influencing committee decisions.
